artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Finally sitting down after getting Ethan’s reading log signed and lunches packed for tomorrow. I wanted to catch you up on a few things, since we only had a few minutes to talk after Sunday dinner in York.

First, do you remember when Jordan got laid off from Capital Heating & Cooling back in August 2018 and we were two months behind on the PPL electric bill and rent here at 218 Meadowbrook Drive? I’ve been thinking about that season a lot lately. We were so close to having to pull Ethan out of his PA PreK Counts classroom at Hillside Elementary in the West Shore School District. I can still picture myself sitting in that little office with the PKC family liaison and then driving over to the Cumberland County Assistance Office, trying not to cry while we figured out how to keep him enrolled and not have our utilities shut off. That whole 2018–2019 school year feels like a blur now.

It’s part of why I’m so obsessed with continuity-of-care policies at work. When I’m coaching teachers or talking to directors about attendance, I’m always thinking about families who hit a sudden crisis like that.

On a lighter note, Ethan’s loving 4th grade at Elmwood. He still talks about his Pre-K teacher more than his current one, which makes me laugh. Jordan’s swamped with HVAC calls again, so we’re juggling pick-up. Could you still do your usual Sunday with us? I’d like to swing by York a little earlier this week to check on your arthritis and Dad’s blood pressure before dinner.

Love you,
Danielle

Hi Melissa,

Just got home from a long day of site visits, but I wanted to write while everything about Ava is still fresh in my mind.

I keep thinking back to that first Head Start year at the Dauphin County Early Learning Center in 2022–2023 when we spent six months—from October through March—pushing for her evaluation through Capital Area Intermediate Unit. It still makes me angry that it took that long after Dr. Lang at UPMC West Shore first raised concerns about possible autism. All those back-and-forth emails between the Head Start team, CAIU, and Dr. Lang, and meanwhile you were just trying to get someone to listen about her speech and sensory stuff.

Now that she’s had the more recent screening at the York County Head Start center (March 2023) and the referral to the pediatric developmental clinic at UPMC Memorial in April, things at least feel more coordinated. I’m glad she finally started weekly OT sessions there in June. Sitting with you in those IFSP meetings, hearing them talk about fine motor delays and sensory strategies, really drives home how confusing this system is for families.

I wrote down notes from today’s IFSP review at UPMC Memorial—what Dr. Patel said about adjusting activities and what the family advocate promised to follow up on. I’ll bring them when we meet at Mom and Dad’s on Sunday so we can go over everything before your next Head Start meeting.

Give Ava a hug from me and tell her Aunt Dani is proud of her “brave work” at therapy.

Love,
Danielle

Hi Karen,

I wanted to debrief a bit after yesterday’s call and also capture some of what’s been swirling in my head.

I keep flashing back to Spring 2023, when our York County PKC partner site had that substantiated child supervision incident in the blended PKC/Head Start classroom on March 14, 2023. Those three months after OCDEL issued the corrective action plan were some of the most intense of my coordinator career—sitting with the site director and the two lead teachers, reworking the staffing schedule line by line, scripting a new transition protocol, and then living with the anxiety of the unannounced follow-up visit. It really showed me how policy decisions land when a program is already under a microscope.

On top of that, I keep thinking about the Lincoln Early Learning Center case in October 2021—the single mom in the Adams County PKC classroom who lost her child’s slot after three late pick-ups past the 4:30 p.m. cutoff because of mandatory overtime at Knouse Foods. She was already on that $6,200 wage garnishment from Gettysburg Hospital, terrified of losing her job. Helping her file an appeal and sit in meetings with the program director and the district solicitor was exhausting, but it also made it impossible for me to see attendance policies as neutral.

Honestly, part of why I’m leaning into more statewide work is that I’m frustrated with how inconsistent and unrealistic some monitoring visits feel at the site level. I want a better line of sight into how decisions get made before they hit us as “non-negotiables.”

Anyway, thanks for always being a sounding board. Let’s talk more at Friday’s Program Quality Committee meeting.

Best,
Danielle

Personal reflection – late-night brain dump

I’ve been thinking about how much of my work lives in the margins of traditional classrooms.

From March 2021 through August 2023, I was at Grace Community Church in York twice a month with Pastor Michael Reynolds, slowly turning that dingy basement fellowship hall into a trauma-informed “Moms in Recovery” childcare room for women referred from WellSpan York Hospital’s substance use treatment program. I still remember May 2022, when Kelsey relapsed and CPS came out to investigate an incident during group. It shook all of us. Using that $2,500 mini-grant from South Central Early Learning Partnership to buy softer lighting, sensory materials, and better storage felt like such a small response to such big pain.

Then there were the Fall 2020 pop-up literacy and ASQ-3 screening events in the parking lot at Kensington Court with the York Housing Authority and CYF—the three chilly Saturday mornings in October and November where I screened about 15 kids, including Ava. Two of the little ones already had open dependency cases, and I wrote “11/18/20 custody hearing at York County Courthouse – Mrs. Ortiz” in my notes so I’d remember to loop in her caseworker.

And that winter and spring of 2019 at the UPMC Pinnacle pediatric clinic on Market Street, running the waiting room literacy corner. I can still feel the weight of the day Dr. Harris asked me to keep a 3-year-old busy for 90 minutes while mom was evaluated for postpartum depression and talked about not wanting to live. That was the first time I really understood secondary trauma.

Sometimes I wonder if I take on too much outside my actual job description.

Hi Jordan,

Quick brain dump while I’m sitting in the car line at Elmwood.

Money stuff first: I know you saw the grocery bill. From January through June this year I’ve been quietly spending around $85–$100 a month at the Giant on Carlisle Pike on diapers, wipes, and gas cards for two PKC families at our Mechanicsburg site. Both have eviction notices hanging over their heads and PPL Electric shut-off warnings. I probably should have told you sooner, but it’s hard to look at those kids and then walk past the diaper aisle.

It all keeps reminding me of early 2021, when you were out of work for five months after your shoulder surgery at UPMC Pinnacle West Shore and we were trying to keep up with our own PPL bill and mortgage. That’s why Parents Supporting Parents–Cumberland felt like the right place to plug in when they asked me to be treasurer in August 2021. We’re still working with a tiny monthly budget—about $3,500—but I’ve already processed more than 60 little micro-grants, mostly $400–$600 to PreK Counts and Head Start families who are behind on rent or facing eviction at the Cumberland County Courthouse. Last fall I even sat on the phone with PPL to help one mom set up a payment plan on a $1,180 past-due balance.

I promise I’m trying to balance the giving with our own emergency fund goals. Maybe we can sit down this weekend and look at the budget together so it doesn’t feel like I’m making those decisions alone.

Ethan just hopped in the car, so I’ll wrap. Tacos tonight?

Love you,
Dani

Journal – committee + burnout thoughts

I’m trying to be honest with myself about these “leadership” things I keep saying yes to.

Families First Early Learning Collaborative has been the bright spot. Since 2020, serving as secretary and chairing Program Quality has felt meaningful. But even there, 2022 was heavy—sitting with Karen at the York County Human Services Center for those three CYF case conferences in March and April after a mandated reporter filed a neglect report on a Head Start family we were backing. We went over the child’s delays, mom’s opioid treatment plan at WellSpan York Hospital, and how the $1,200 emergency fund we approved was literally keeping child care afloat while the investigation played out.

PennAEYC’s Public Policy Committee (2019–June 2023) was different. I’m proud of the Fall 2021 brief we worked on about protecting PreK Counts slots for kids in child welfare and juvenile justice. I shared Ava’s story—with Melissa’s blessing—about her biological dad Mark’s domestic violence arrest and the October 2020 York County family court hearing that ended his visits, and how that chaos wrecked her attendance at the York City Head Start site. But if I’m honest, on a lot of those statewide Zooms I mostly sat on mute listening. The policy folks sounded so sure of themselves, and I rarely pushed my way into the conversation.

Lately, I’m wondering if I should be more selective. I was even asked to step off a tiny local nonprofit board last year after missing too many meetings when work got wild. That stung more than I expected.

I don’t want to keep overcommitting and under-showing up.

[Thread with friend Carla]

Danielle: You would laugh if you saw my calendar right now. Between my own sites and all these “extras,” I’m basically living in my car.

Carla: What’s going on this week?

Danielle: Tomorrow I’m at the Mechanicsburg Community Resource Center for the re-entry parent–child playgroup. Remember that thing I ran from Sept 2017 to May 2018 for families referred from Dauphin County Prison? We’re doing a reunion session and Mark Delaney from probation is coming. I still think about the March 2018 night when one dad showed up clearly drunk and I had to ask him to leave and then email Mark a violation report.

Carla: Yikes. That was rough.

Danielle: Yeah. And then Thursday I’ve got a meeting with CYS about a PreK Counts kid whose dad is at Camp Hill. I’ve been the point person since August 2021, trying to sync supervised visitation, our classroom safety plan, and the court order. It’s a lot of systems for one 4-year-old.

Carla: You do so much. Do you ever get to just chill?

Danielle: Honestly? Not really. I even covered for a friend’s unlicensed home daycare a few mornings last spring when she had appointments. I knew her ratios and paperwork weren’t exactly OCDEL-approved, but I still said yes. Plus I tried running a little church-basement playgroup a while back and totally burned out on the planning.

Carla: You need boundaries, lady.

Danielle: I know. Maybe after this round of kindergarten transition events is over I’ll actually block off a no-volunteer month.

Event: Therapy with Dr. Ellis – secondary trauma check-in

Date: Thursday, 2023-11-16
Time: 4:30–5:30 p.m.
Location: Penn State Health Hampden Medical Center, Enola – Behavioral Health, 3rd floor
Attendees: Danielle; Dr. Karen Ellis

Notes:

Leave Mechanicsburg PKC site by 3:45 p.m. so I’m not racing up 81. Ask Jordan to grab Ethan from Elmwood Elementary and start homework.

Things to bring up with Dr. Ellis:

– The Hanover case from the Lincoln Intermediate Unit 12 Kindergarten Transition Task Force pilot (2022–2023). I’m still replaying that March 2023 case review at the LIU12 office for the 5-year-old from the Hanover Head Start classroom whose dad is incarcerated at SCI Camp Hill. We went through his February 3-day suspension for aggressive behavior, the behavior incident logs, and the TrueNorth Wellness play therapy notes with the Hanover Street Elementary counselor. I keep thinking about whether we did enough to prepare the school.

– How therapy is helping me show up for Ethan. Since the bullying incident on the Elmwood playground in November 2022, his school refusal and anxiety spikes any time there’s a substitute teacher. Working with Ms. Rachel King on his gradual re-entry and 504 plan has been good, but it’s exhausting to be “professional Danielle” for other families and “mom Danielle” for him.

– My patience level with slow, bureaucratic meetings. I’m finding it harder to sit through two-hour regional calls that go nowhere without wanting to scream.

Self-care after: short walk around the townhouse development, no email.

metadata.title: Therapy w/ Dr. Ellis
metadata.location: Penn State Health Hampden Medical Center, Enola, PA

artifacts.content:
From: Danielle Carter <dcarter@scearlylearning.org>
To: Self <danielle.carter.personal@gmail.com>
Date: 2022-04-22 9:14 PM
Subject: Notes for portfolio – “other settings” experience

Draft for PDE portfolio – DO NOT SEND YET

Practical Experiences in Other Setting That Is Not Listed – Please Describe

During Spring 2022, I supported a small in‑home family child care provider in York who is also my former neighbor, Carla Jenkins. Carla operates a family child care program out of a rented duplex on West Philadelphia Street, York, PA. The work was informal (unpaid) but fairly intensive over several weeks.

My role was to help her get ready to apply for Keystone STARS. I coached her on basic documentation systems (attendance, incident reports, developmental notes), and we walked through the family engagement indicators together. A big complicating factor was that she had lost several child care clients during COVID, which left her almost $3,000 in back rent.

Because of that, Carla received two eviction notices in March 2022 and April 2022. She was at real risk of eviction and program closure. With her permission, I reviewed copies of both eviction notices so I could accurately reference dates, amounts owed, and her payment history.

We then drafted hardship letters to her landlord and to the local Early Learning Resource Center (ELRC). The goal was to document the COVID‑related loss of income and request temporary subsidy relief and a payment plan so she could remain in the duplex and keep her in‑home program open. This experience deepened my understanding of how housing instability intersects with quality improvement work for family child care providers.

Apple Notes – Personal/Work Blend
Title: Carla J – follow‑up + resources
Last edited: 2022-04-05, 6:38 AM

Context: Former neighbor, now running in‑home family child care on West Philadelphia Street, York, PA. Spring 2022 support has gotten more intense than I expected, but it’s the right thing.

To‑do – week of April 4–8, 2022

• Re‑read Carla Jenkins’ April 2022 eviction notice (compare to March 2022 one) – double‑check total showing almost $3,000 in back rent.
• Outline hardship letter language for landlord: emphasize loss of several child care clients during COVID, impact on her ability to keep the program open, and her history of on‑time payments pre‑pandemic.
• Draft separate hardship letter to local Early Learning Resource Center (ELRC) requesting temporary subsidy relief + possible back‑pay arrangement so she doesn’t have to shut down.
• Create simple one‑page checklist for Carla: Keystone STARS documentation she already has vs. what’s missing (family handbooks, communication logs, health/safety postings).
• Look up any York City resources that address risk of eviction and program closure for small providers – maybe United Way or housing stability grants she could reference in the letters.
• Add anonymized version of this situation to my “system barriers” notes for future family engagement/advocacy trainings – powerful example of how housing insecurity collides with quality expectations.

Reminder to self: This is exactly the kind of behind‑the‑scenes crisis families/providers are juggling when we wonder why forms are late or CLASS scores dip. Don’t lose that perspective.
